revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Otori Taisha
大鳥神社 | image = http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200081.jpg | width = | caption = | type = | dedication = Prince Yamato Takeru | founded = ? | closed = | founder = ? | priest = | address = [[Osaka-fu]], [[Sakai-shi]], [[Nishi-ku]], [[Otorikita-Machi]] 1-1-2 | phone = 072-262-0040 | website = http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html }} '''{{nihongo|Otori Grand Shrine|大鳥神社|Otori-taisha}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishi-ku]] ward in the city of [[Sakai]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The legend of the origin of the shrine states that the hero Yamatotakeru-no-Mikoto, who turned into a white swan upon his death, last stopped at the future site of the Otori Taisha. It is said that the people nearby built the shrine in order to worship white swanshttp://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html. The prince [[Yamato Takeru]] (originially prince Ousu), son of Emperor Keiko of the Yamato Dynasty, is enshrined here. The prince was praised for his bravery in battle and was given the name Yamato Takeru, or The Brave of Yamato, by his enemy as he died http://abikobc.blog59.fc2.com/blog-entry-1141.html. Otori Taisha is believed to be in the service of the gods of literature and martial arts, and it was said that warriors often visited in ancient times. ==Grounds== The shrine's grounds, which extend over 50,000 square meters, contain a quiet forest known as Chigusa-no-mori because of the seemingly endless variety of tree species which can be found there. The shrine is also known for it's iris garden, which features over 100,000 flowers. There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tessai Tomioka]]. ==Notable Architecture== The main building, Otori Zukuri, is built in one of the oldest architectural styles used for Japanese shrineshttp://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg. This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. To reach Otori Taisha, take the JR Hanwa Line and get off at Otori Station. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html Sakai City Site]

The prince was praised for his bravery in battle and was given the name Yamato Takeru, or The Brave of Yamato, by his enemy as he died http://abikobc.blog59.fc2.com/blog-entry-1141.html. Otori Taisha is believed to be in the service of the gods of literature and martial arts, and it was said that warriors often visited in ancient times. ==Grounds== The shrine's grounds, which extend over 50,000 square meters, contain a quiet forest known as Chigusa-no-mori because of the seemingly endless variety of tree species which can be found there. The shrine is also known for it's iris garden, which features over 100,000 flowers. There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tessai Tomioka]]. ==Notable Architecture== The main building, Otori Zukuri, is built in one of the oldest architectural styles used for Japanese shrineshttp://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg. This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ōtori Taisha
大鳥神社 | image = Otori-taisha.jpg | width = | caption = Inside Ōtori Taisha | type = | dedication = Prince Yamato Takeru | founded = ? | closed = | founder = ? | priest = | address = [[Osaka-fu]], [[Sakai-shi]], [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[Otorikita-Machi]] 1-1-2 | phone = 072-262-0040 | website = http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html }} '''{{nihongo|Ōtori Grand Shrine|大鳥神社|Ōtori-taisha}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]] ward in the city of [[Sakai, Osaka|Sakai]], [[Osaka]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The legend of the origin of the shrine states that the hero Yamatotakeru-no-Mikoto, who turned into a white swan upon his death, last stopped at the future site of the Otori Taisha. It is said that the people nearby built the shrine in order to worship white swanshttp://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html. The prince [[Yamato Takeru]] (originally prince Ousu), son of Emperor Keiko of the Yamato Dynasty, is enshrined here. The prince was praised for his bravery in battle and was given the name Yamato Takeru, or The Brave of Yamato, by his enemy as he died http://abikobc.blog59.fc2.com/blog-entry-1141.html. Ōtori Taisha is believed to be in the service of the gods of literature and martial arts, and it was said that warriors often visited in ancient times. ==Grounds== The shrine's grounds, which extend over 50,000 square meters, contain a quiet forest known as Chigusa-no-mori because of the seemingly endless variety of tree species which can be found there. The shrine is also known for its iris garden, which features over 100,000 flowers. There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tessai Tomioka]]. ==Notable Architecture== The main building, Ōtori Zukuri, is built in one of the oldest architectural styles used for Japanese shrineshttp://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg. This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. To reach Ōtori Taisha, take the JR Hanwa Line and get off at Otori Station. ==Gallery== Image:OtoriEntrance.JPG|Entrance to Ōtori Taisha. Image:OtoriTaisha.JPG|Inside Ōtori Taisha. Image:Otorishrine.JPG|Inside Ōtori Taisha. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html Sakai City Site] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:大鳥大社]]

revision.comment: Kanpei taisha, 1871-1946
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ōtori taisha
大鳥神社 | image = Otori-taisha.jpg | width = | caption = Inside Ōtori taisha | type = | dedication = Prince Yamato Takeru | founded = ? | closed = | founder = ? | priest = | address = [[Osaka-fu]], [[Sakai-shi]], [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[Otorikita-Machi]] 1-1-2 | phone = 072-262-0040 | website = http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori taisha'''|大鳥神社|}}, also known as Ōtori Grand Shrine, is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]] ward in the city of [[Sakai, Osaka|Sakai]], [[Osaka]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The legend of the origin of the shrine states that the hero Yamatotakeru-no-Mikoto, who turned into a white swan upon his death, last stopped at the future site of the Otori Taisha. It is said that the people nearby built the shrine in order to worship white swanshttp://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html. The prince [[Yamato Takeru]] (originally prince Ousu), son of Emperor Keiko of the Yamato Dynasty, is enshrined here. The prince was praised for his bravery in battle and was given the name Yamato Takeru, or The Brave of Yamato, by his enemy as he died http://abikobc.blog59.fc2.com/blog-entry-1141.html. Ōtori Taisha is believed to be in the service of the gods of literature and martial arts, and it was said that warriors often visited in ancient times. Ōtori was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Izumi province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Ōtori was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. ==Grounds== The shrine's grounds, which extend over 50,000 square meters, contain a quiet forest known as Chigusa-no-mori because of the seemingly endless variety of tree species which can be found there. The shrine is also known for its iris garden, which features over 100,000 flowers. There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tessai Tomioka]]. ==Notable Architecture== The main building, Ōtori zukuri, is built in one of the oldest architectural styles used for Japanese shrineshttp://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg. This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. To reach Ōtori taisha, take the JR Hanwa Line and get off at Otori Station. ==Gallery== Image:OtoriEntrance.JPG|Entrance to Ōtori taisha. Image:OtoriTaisha.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. Image:Otorishrine.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html Sakai City Site] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|34|32|13|N|135|27|39|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otori Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:大鳥大社]]

The shrine is also known for its iris garden, which features over 100,000 flowers. There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tessai Tomioka]]. ==Notable Architecture== The main building, Ōtori zukuri, is built in one of the oldest [http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shinto_architecture&oldid=444272237#.C5.8Ctori-zukuri architectural styles used for Japanese shrines].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. Ōtori was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Izumi province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Ōtori was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. ==Grounds== The shrine's grounds, which extend over 50,000 square meters, contain a quiet forest known as Chigusa-no-mori because of the seemingly endless variety of tree species which can be found there. The shrine is also known for its iris garden, which features over 100,000 flowers. There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tomioka Tessai]]. ==Notable Architecture== The main building, Ōtori zukuri, is built in one of the oldest [http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shinto_architecture&oldid=444272237#.C5.8Ctori-zukuri architectural styles used for Japanese shrines].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. To reach Ōtori taisha, take the JR Hanwa Line and get off at Otori Station. ==Gallery== Image:OtoriEntrance.JPG|Entrance to Ōtori taisha. Image:OtoriTaisha.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. Image:Otorishrine.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html Sakai City Site] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|34|32|13|N|135|27|39|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otori Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Osaka]] [[fr:Ōtori-taisha]] [[ja:大鳥大社]] [[zh:大鳥大社]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōtori taisha
大鳥神社 | image = Otori-taisha.jpg | caption = Inside Ōtori taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|13|N|135|27|39|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Prince Yamato Takeru | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Osaka-fu]], [[Sakai-shi]], [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[Otorikita-Machi]] 1-1-2 | website = {{URL|http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori taisha'''|大鳥神社|}}, also known as Ōtori Grand Shrine, is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]] ward in the city of [[Sakai, Osaka|Sakai]], [[Osaka]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The legend of the origin of the shrine states that the hero [[Yamato Takeru|Yamatotakeru-no-Mikoto]], who turned into a white swan upon his death, last stopped at the future site of the Otori Taisha. It is said that the people nearby built the shrine in order to worship white swans.http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html The prince [[Yamato Takeru]] (originally prince Ousu), son of [[Emperor Keiko]] of the [[Yamato Dynasty]], is enshrined here. The prince was praised for his bravery in battle and was given the name Yamato Takeru, or The Brave of Yamato, by his enemy as he died.http://abikobc.blog59.fc2.com/blog-entry-1141.html Ōtori Taisha is believed to be in the service of the gods of literature and martial arts, and it was said that warriors often visited in ancient times. Ōtori was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Izumi province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Ōtori was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. ==Grounds== The shrine's grounds, which extend over 50,000 square meters, contain a quiet forest known as Chigusa-no-mori because of the seemingly endless variety of tree species which can be found there. The shrine is also known for its iris garden, which features over 100,000 flowers. There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tomioka Tessai]]. ==Notable Architecture== The main building, Ōtori zukuri, is built in one of the oldest [[Shinto architecture|architectural styles]] used for Japanese shrines.http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. To reach Ōtori taisha, take the JR Hanwa Line and get off at Otori Station. ==Gallery== Image:OtoriEntrance.JPG|Entrance to Ōtori taisha. Image:OtoriTaisha.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. Image:Otorishrine.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. == See also== {{commons category|Ootori-taisha, Osaka}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html Sakai City Site] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otori Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Osaka]]

The shrine is also known for its iris garden, which features over 100,000 flowers. To reach Ōtori taisha, take the JR Hanwa Line and get off at Otori Station. ==Gallery== Image:OtoriEntrance.JPG|Entrance to Ōtori taisha. Image:OtoriTaisha.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. Image:Otorishrine.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. == See also== {{commons category|Ootori-taisha, Osaka}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html Sakai City Site] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otori Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Osaka]]

There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tomioka Tessai]]. ==Notable Architecture== The main building, Ōtori zukuri, is built in one of the oldest [[Shinto architecture|architectural styles]] used for Japanese shrines.http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Festival== A traditional festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October at around this shrine.The festival is called ”Danjiri Matsuri". Image:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Danjiri Matsuri Image:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 001.webm|Danjiri Matsuri ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. To reach Ōtori taisha, take the JR Hanwa Line and get off at Otori Station. ==Gallery== Image:OtoriEntrance.JPG|Entrance to Ōtori taisha. Image:OtoriTaisha.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. Image:Otorishrine.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. == See also== {{Portal|Japan}} {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html Sakai City Site] * {{OSM|w|238183992}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otori Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Osaka]]

There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tomioka Tessai]]. ==Notable Architecture== The main building, Ōtori zukuri, is built in one of the oldest [[Shinto architecture|architectural styles]] used for Japanese shrines.http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Festival== A traditional festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October at around this shrine.The festival is called ”Danjiri Matsuri". File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 001.webm|Danjiri Matsuri ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. To reach Ōtori taisha, take the JR Hanwa Line and get off at Otori Station. ==Gallery== Image:OtoriEntrance.JPG|Entrance to Ōtori taisha. Image:OtoriTaisha.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. Image:Otorishrine.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. == See also== {{Portal|Japan}} {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot2.html Sakai City Site] * {{OSM|w|238183992}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otori Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Osaka]]

It is said that the people nearby built the shrine in order to worship white swans.https://www.sakai-tcb.or.jp/spot/detail/40 The prince [[Yamato Takeru]] (originally prince Ousu), son of [[Emperor Keiko]] of the [[Yamato Dynasty]], is enshrined here. The prince was praised for his bravery in battle and was given the name Yamato Takeru, or The Brave of Yamato, by his enemy as he died.https://orias.berkeley.edu/yamato-plot-summary Ōtori Taisha is believed to be in the service of the gods of literature and martial arts, and it was said that warriors often visited in ancient times. Ōtori was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Izumi province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Ōtori was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. ==Grounds== The shrine's grounds, which extend over 50,000 square meters, contain a quiet forest known as Chigusa-no-mori because of the seemingly endless variety of tree species which can be found there. The shrine is also known for its iris garden, which features over 100,000 flowers. There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tomioka Tessai]]. ==Notable architecture== The main building is build in the [[Shinto architecture#Ōtori-zukuri|Ōtori zukuri]] style, one of the oldest [[Shinto architecture|architectural styles]] used for Japanese shrines.https://en.japantravel.com/osaka/otori-taisha/65639 This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Festival== A traditional festival, [[Danjiri Matsuri]], is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October at around this shrine. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 001.webm|Danjiri Matsuri ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. To reach Ōtori taisha, take the JR Hanwa Line and get off at Otori Station. ==Gallery== Image:OtoriEntrance.JPG|Entrance to Ōtori taisha. Image:OtoriTaisha.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. Image:Otorishrine.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. == See also== {{Portal|Japan}} {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} * {{OSM|w|238183992}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otori Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Osaka]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sakai, Nara, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōtori taisha
大鳥神社 | image = Otori-taisha.jpg | caption = Inside Ōtori taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|13|N|135|27|39|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Prince Yamato Takeru | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Osaka-fu]], [[Sakai-shi]], [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[Otorikita-Machi]] 1-1-2 | website = {{URL|https://www.sakai-tcb.or.jp/spot/detail/40}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori taisha'''|大鳥神社|}}, also known as '''Ōtori Grand Shrine''', is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]] ward in the city of [[Sakai, Osaka|Sakai]], [[Osaka]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The legend of the origin of the shrine states that the hero [[Yamato Takeru|Yamatotakeru-no-Mikoto]], who turned into a white swan upon his death, last stopped at the future site of the Otori Taisha. It is said that the people nearby built the shrine in order to worship white swans.{{Cite web|url=https://www.sakai-tcb.or.jp/spot/detail/40|title=大鳥大社｜スポット｜堺観光ガイド|website=堺観光ガイド}} The prince [[Yamato Takeru]] (originally prince Ousu), son of [[Emperor Keiko]] of the [[Yamato Dynasty]], is enshrined here. The prince was praised for his bravery in battle and was given the name Yamato Takeru, or The Brave of Yamato, by his enemy as he died.{{Cite web|url=https://orias.berkeley.edu/yamato-plot-summary|title=Yamato Plot Summary | ORIAS|website=orias.berkeley.edu}} Ōtori Taisha is believed to be in the service of the gods of literature and martial arts, and it was said that warriors often visited in ancient times. Ōtori was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Izumi province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Ōtori was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. ==Grounds== The shrine's grounds, which extend over 50,000 square meters, contain a quiet forest known as Chigusa-no-mori because of the seemingly endless variety of tree species which can be found there. The shrine is also known for its iris garden, which features over 100,000 flowers. There is a monument in the shrine on which a tanka poem was inscribed by [[Tomioka Tessai]]. ==Notable architecture== The main building is build in the [[Shinto architecture#Ōtori-zukuri|Ōtori zukuri]] style, one of the oldest [[Shinto architecture|architectural styles]] used for Japanese shrines.{{Cite web|url=https://en.japantravel.com/osaka/otori-taisha/65639|title=Otori Taisha - Sakai City, Osaka|website=JapanTravel}} This building burned down in 1905, but was rebuilt in 1909. ==Festival== A traditional festival, [[Danjiri Matsuri]], is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October at around this shrine. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 001.webm|Danjiri Matsuri ==Visiting== There are no set hours and admission is free. To reach Ōtori taisha, take the JR Hanwa Line and get off at Otori Station. ==Gallery== Image:OtoriEntrance.JPG|Entrance to Ōtori taisha. Image:OtoriTaisha.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. Image:Otorishrine.JPG|Inside Ōtori taisha. == See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} * {{OSM|w|238183992}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otori Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Osaka]]

revision.comment: expand
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sakai, Osaka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōtori taisha
大鳥大社 | image = Ootori-taisha haiden.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and [[Yamato Takeru]] | founded_by = | established = c.[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-2 Ōtorikita-machi, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''Ōtori-zukuri'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[Sakai]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Izumi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13. {{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of [[Mount Ibuki]] and died in [[Ise Province]]. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|burial mound]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of [[Emperor Keikō]] and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||} which is believed to be a very ancient style of [[Shinto architecture]], second only to the style of [[Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by [[Gyōki]] in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the [[Five regent houses]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the [[Sengoku period]], [[Oda Nobunaga]] confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''[[koku]]'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of [[Toyotomi Hideyori]]. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 [[Siege of Osaka]]. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''[[Sakai bugyō]]'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by [[Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]]. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the [[Kansai region]]. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''[[kokugaku]]'' studies towards the [[Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[Otori Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Hanwa Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ootori-taisha 20040313 001.jpg|Torii Otori-taisha, shaden.jpg|Honden Yamato Takeru(bronze statue,Osaka)02.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''[[Danjiri Matsuri]]’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 001.webm|Danjiri Matsuri ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sakai, Osaka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōtori taisha
大鳥大社 | image = Ootori-taisha haiden.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and [[Yamato Takeru]] | founded_by = | established = c.[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-2 Ōtorikita-machi, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''Ōtori-zukuri'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[Sakai]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Izumi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of [[Mount Ibuki]] and died in [[Ise Province]]. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|burial mound]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of [[Emperor Keikō]] and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||} which is believed to be a very ancient style of [[Shinto architecture]], second only to the style of [[Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by [[Gyōki]] in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the [[Five regent houses]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the [[Sengoku period]], [[Oda Nobunaga]] confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''[[koku]]'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of [[Toyotomi Hideyori]]. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 [[Siege of Osaka]]. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''[[Sakai bugyō]]'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by [[Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]]. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the [[Kansai region]]. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''[[kokugaku]]'' studies towards the [[Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[Otori Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Hanwa Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ootori-taisha 20040313 001.jpg|Torii Otori-taisha, shaden.jpg|Honden Yamato Takeru(bronze statue,Osaka)02.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''[[Danjiri Matsuri]]’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 001.webm|Danjiri Matsuri ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sakai, Osaka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōtori taisha
大鳥大社 | image = Ootori-taisha haiden.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and [[Yamato Takeru]] | founded_by = | established = c.[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-2 Ōtorikita-machi, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''Ōtori-zukuri'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[Sakai]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Izumi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} [[Category:Beppo Shrines‎]] ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of [[Mount Ibuki]] and died in [[Ise Province]]. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|burial mound]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of [[Emperor Keikō]] and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan [[Category:Kanpo-taisya]] ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||} which is believed to be a very ancient style of [[Shinto architecture]], second only to the style of [[Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by [[Gyōki]] in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the [[Five regent houses]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the [[Sengoku period]], [[Oda Nobunaga]] confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''[[koku]]'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of [[Toyotomi Hideyori]]. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 [[Siege of Osaka]]. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''[[Sakai bugyō]]'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by [[Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]]. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the [[Kansai region]]. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''[[kokugaku]]'' studies towards the [[Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[Otori Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Hanwa Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ootori-taisha 20040313 001.jpg|Torii Otori-taisha, shaden.jpg|Honden Yamato Takeru(bronze statue,Osaka)02.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''[[Danjiri Matsuri]]’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 001.webm|Danjiri Matsuri ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sakai, Osaka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōtori taisha
大鳥大社 | image = Ootori-taisha haiden.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and [[Yamato Takeru]] | founded_by = | established = c.[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-2 Ōtorikita-machi, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''Ōtori-zukuri'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[Sakai]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Izumi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of [[Mount Ibuki]] and died in [[Ise Province]]. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|burial mound]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of [[Emperor Keikō]] and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of [[Shinto architecture]], second only to the style of [[Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by [[Gyōki]] in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the [[Five regent houses]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the [[Sengoku period]], [[Oda Nobunaga]] confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''[[koku]]'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of [[Toyotomi Hideyori]]. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 [[Siege of Osaka]]. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''[[Sakai bugyō]]'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by [[Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]]. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the [[Kansai region]]. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''[[kokugaku]]'' studies towards the [[Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[Otori Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Hanwa Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ootori-taisha 20040313 001.jpg|Torii Otori-taisha, shaden.jpg|Honden Yamato Takeru(bronze statue,Osaka)02.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''[[Danjiri Matsuri]]’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 001.webm|Danjiri Matsuri ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpo-taisya]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]]

revision.comment: Moving [[:Category:Kanpo-taisya]] to [[:Category:Kanpei-taisha]] per [[Wikipedia:Categories for discussion/Speedy]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sakai, Osaka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōtori taisha
大鳥大社 | image = Ootori-taisha haiden.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and [[Yamato Takeru]] | founded_by = | established = c.[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-2 Ōtorikita-machi, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''Ōtori-zukuri'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[Sakai]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Izumi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of [[Mount Ibuki]] and died in [[Ise Province]]. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|burial mound]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of [[Emperor Keikō]] and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of [[Shinto architecture]], second only to the style of [[Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by [[Gyōki]] in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the [[Five regent houses]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the [[Sengoku period]], [[Oda Nobunaga]] confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''[[koku]]'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of [[Toyotomi Hideyori]]. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 [[Siege of Osaka]]. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''[[Sakai bugyō]]'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by [[Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]]. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the [[Kansai region]]. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''[[kokugaku]]'' studies towards the [[Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[Otori Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Hanwa Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ootori-taisha 20040313 001.jpg|Torii Otori-taisha, shaden.jpg|Honden Yamato Takeru(bronze statue,Osaka)02.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''[[Danjiri Matsuri]]’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 009.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 007.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 005.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 006.webm ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]]

The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''[[kokugaku]]'' studies towards the [[Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[Otori Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Hanwa Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ootori-taisha 20040313 001.jpg|Torii Otori-taisha, shaden.jpg|Honden Yamato Takeru(bronze statue,Osaka)02.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''[[Danjiri Matsuri]]’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 009.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 007.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 005.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 006.webm ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sakai, Osaka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōtori taisha
大鳥大社 | image = Ootori-taisha haiden.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and [[Yamato Takeru]] | founded_by = | established = c.[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-2 Ōtorikita-machi, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''Ōtori-zukuri'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[Sakai]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Izumi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of [[Mount Ibuki]] and died in [[Ise Province]]. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|burial mound]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of [[Emperor Keikō]] and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of [[Shinto architecture]], second only to the style of [[Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by [[Gyōki]] in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the [[Five regent houses]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the [[Sengoku period]], [[Oda Nobunaga]] confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''[[koku]]'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of [[Toyotomi Hideyori]]. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 [[Siege of Osaka]]. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''[[Sakai bugyō]]'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by [[Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]]. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the [[Kansai region]]. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''[[kokugaku]]'' studies towards the [[Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated a {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[Otori Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Hanwa Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ootori-taisha 20040313 001.jpg|Torii Otori-taisha, shaden.jpg|Honden Yamato Takeru(bronze statue,Osaka)02.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''[[Danjiri Matsuri]]’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 009.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 007.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 005.webm File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 006.webm ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]]
