大鳥大社 | image = Ōtori-taisha, Worship Hall 001.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] | founded_by = | established = c.[[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-2 Ōtorikita-machi 1-chō, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu 593-8328, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''{{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori-zukuri|1=Ōtori-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441991}}'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Nishi-ku, Sakai|Nishi-ku]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of [[:en:Mount Ibuki|Mount Ibuki]] and died in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|Kofun]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto architecture]], second only to the style of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyori|Toyotomi Hideyori]]. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 [[:en:Siege of Osaka|Siege of Osaka]]. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''[[:en:Sakai bugyō|Sakai bugyō]]'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]]. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]]. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' studies towards the [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[:en:Otori Station|Otori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Ōtori-taisha, A Torii Gate at the Entrance to a Shrine 001.jpg|A torii gate at the entrance of a shrine File:Ōtori-taisha, Principal Shrine 001.jpg|Honden File:Ōtori-taisha, Statue of Yamato Takeru 001.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''[[:en:Danjiri Matsuri|Danjiri Matsuri]]’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 009.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 007.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 005.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 006.webm ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] ‎ {{Ōtori Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Ōtori taisha]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} [[fr:Ōtori-taisha]] [[ja:大鳥大社]] [[zh:大鳥大社]] {{wikidata link|Q705151}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]]

It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of {{ill|Mount Ibuki|en|Mount Ibuki|WD=Q2624360|lt=Mount Ibuki}} and died in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|Kofun]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture}}, second only to the style of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}}'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki}} in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|WD=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori}}. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''{{ill|Sakai bugyō|en|Sakai bugyō|WD=Q4274622|lt=Sakai bugyō}}'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' studies towards the [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[:en:Otori Station|Otori Station]] on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hanwa Line|en|Hanwa Line|WD=Q1154800|lt=Hanwa Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Ōtori-taisha, A Torii Gate at the Entrance to a Shrine 001.jpg|A torii gate at the entrance of a shrine File:Ōtori-taisha, Principal Shrine 001.jpg|Honden File:Ōtori-taisha, Statue of Yamato Takeru 001.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''{{ill|Danjiri Matsuri|en|Danjiri Matsuri|WD=Q5219928|lt=Danjiri Matsuri}}’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 009.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 007.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 005.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 006.webm ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] ‎ {{Ōtori Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Ōtori taisha]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} [[fr:Ōtori-taisha]] [[ja:大鳥大社]] [[zh:大鳥大社]] {{wikidata link|Q705151}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]]

大鳥大社 | image = Ōtori-taisha, Worship Hall 001.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} | founded_by = | established = c.{{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} | date_destroyed = | location = 1-2 Ōtorikita-machi 1-chō, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu 593-8328, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''{{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori-zukuri|1=Ōtori-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441991}}'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nishi-ku, Sakai|en|Nishi-ku, Sakai|WD=Q1042499|lt=Nishi-ku}}, {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of {{ill|Mount Ibuki|en|Mount Ibuki|WD=Q2624360|lt=Mount Ibuki}} and died in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|Kofun]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture}}, second only to the style of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}}'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki}} in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|WD=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori}}. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''{{ill|Sakai bugyō|en|Sakai bugyō|WD=Q4274622|lt=Sakai bugyō}}'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' studies towards the [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[:en:Otori Station|Otori Station]] on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hanwa Line|en|Hanwa Line|WD=Q1154800|lt=Hanwa Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Ōtori-taisha, A Torii Gate at the Entrance to a Shrine 001.jpg|A torii gate at the entrance of a shrine File:Ōtori-taisha, Principal Shrine 001.jpg|Honden File:Ōtori-taisha, Statue of Yamato Takeru 001.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''{{ill|Danjiri Matsuri|en|Danjiri Matsuri|WD=Q5219928|lt=Danjiri Matsuri}}’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 009.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 007.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 005.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 006.webm ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] ‎ {{Ōtori Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Ōtori taisha]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} [[fr:Ōtori-taisha]] [[ja:大鳥大社]] [[zh:大鳥大社]] {{wikidata link|Q705151}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]]

It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of {{ill|Mount Ibuki|en|Mount Ibuki|WD=Q2624360|lt=Mount Ibuki}} and died in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|Kofun]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture}}, second only to the style of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}}'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki}} in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|WD=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori}}. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''{{ill|Sakai bugyō|en|Sakai bugyō|WD=Q4274622|lt=Sakai bugyō}}'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' studies towards the [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[:en:Otori Station|Otori Station]] on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hanwa Line|en|Hanwa Line|WD=Q1154800|lt=Hanwa Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Ōtori-taisha, A Torii Gate at the Entrance to a Shrine 001.jpg|A torii gate at the entrance of a shrine File:Ōtori-taisha, Principal Shrine 001.jpg|Honden File:Ōtori-taisha, Statue of Yamato Takeru 001.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''{{ill|Danjiri Matsuri|en|Danjiri Matsuri|WD=Q5219928|lt=Danjiri Matsuri}}’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 009.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 007.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 005.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 006.webm ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] ‎ {{Ōtori Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Ōtori taisha]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} [[fr:Ōtori-taisha]] [[ja:大鳥大社]] [[zh:大鳥大社]] {{wikidata link|Q705151}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]]

大鳥大社 | image = Ōtori-taisha, Worship Hall 001.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} | founded_by = | established = c.{{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} | date_destroyed = | location = 1-2 Ōtorikita-machi 1-chō, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu 593-8328, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''{{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori-zukuri|1=Ōtori-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441991}}'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nishi-ku, Sakai|en|Nishi-ku, Sakai|WD=Q1042499|lt=Nishi-ku}}, {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} mythology. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|Kofun]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture}}, second only to the style of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}}'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki}} in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|WD=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori}}. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''{{ill|Sakai bugyō|en|Sakai bugyō|WD=Q4274622|lt=Sakai bugyō}}'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' studies towards the [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. 大鳥大社 | image = Ōtori-taisha, Worship Hall 001.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} | founded_by = | established = c.{{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} | date_destroyed = | location = 1-2 Ōtorikita-machi 1-chō, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu 593-8328, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''{{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori-zukuri|1=Ōtori-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441991}}'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nishi-ku, Sakai|en|Nishi-ku, Sakai|WD=Q1042499|lt=Nishi-ku}}, {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of {{ill|Mount Ibuki|en|Mount Ibuki|WD=Q2624360|lt=Mount Ibuki}} and died in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|Kofun]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture}}, second only to the style of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}}'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki}} in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|WD=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori}}. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''{{ill|Sakai bugyō|en|Sakai bugyō|WD=Q4274622|lt=Sakai bugyō}}'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' studies towards the [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[:en:Otori Station|Otori Station]] on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hanwa Line|en|Hanwa Line|WD=Q1154800|lt=Hanwa Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Ōtori-taisha, A Torii Gate at the Entrance to a Shrine 001.jpg|A torii gate at the entrance of a shrine File:Ōtori-taisha, Principal Shrine 001.jpg|Honden File:Ōtori-taisha, Statue of Yamato Takeru 001.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''{{ill|Danjiri Matsuri|en|Danjiri Matsuri|WD=Q5219928|lt=Danjiri Matsuri}}’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 009.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 007.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 005.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 006.webm ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] ‎ {{Ōtori Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Ōtori taisha]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} [[fr:Ōtori-taisha]] [[ja:大鳥大社]] [[zh:大鳥大社]] {{wikidata link|Q705151}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. 大鳥大社 | image = Ōtori-taisha, Worship Hall 001.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} | founded_by = | established = c.{{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} | date_destroyed = | location = 1-2 Ōtorikita-machi 1-chō, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu 593-8328, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''{{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori-zukuri|1=Ōtori-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441991}}'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nishi-ku, Sakai|en|Nishi-ku, Sakai|WD=Q1042499|lt=Nishi-ku}}, {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of {{ill|Mount Ibuki|en|Mount Ibuki|WD=Q2624360|lt=Mount Ibuki}} and died in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|Kofun]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture}}, second only to the style of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}}'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki}} in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|WD=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori}}. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''{{ill|Sakai bugyō|en|Sakai bugyō|WD=Q4274622|lt=Sakai bugyō}}'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' studies towards the [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[:en:Otori Station|Otori Station]] on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hanwa Line|en|Hanwa Line|WD=Q1154800|lt=Hanwa Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Ōtori-taisha, A Torii Gate at the Entrance to a Shrine 001.jpg|A torii gate at the entrance of a shrine File:Ōtori-taisha, Principal Shrine 001.jpg|Honden File:Ōtori-taisha, Statue of Yamato Takeru 001.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''{{ill|Danjiri Matsuri|en|Danjiri Matsuri|WD=Q5219928|lt=Danjiri Matsuri}}’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 009.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 007.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 005.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 006.webm ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] ‎ {{Ōtori Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Ōtori taisha]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} [[fr:Ōtori-taisha]] [[ja:大鳥大社]] [[zh:大鳥大社]] {{wikidata link|Q705151}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 009.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 007.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 005.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 006.webm ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] ‎ {{Ōtori Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Ōtori taisha]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} [[fr:Ōtori-taisha]] [[ja:大鳥大社]] [[zh:大鳥大社]] {{wikidata link|Q705151}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[[co:commons:Category:Ōtori-taisha]][[en:Ōtori taisha]][[fr:Ōtori-taisha]][[ja:大鳥大社]][[zh:大鳥大社]] [[co:commons:Category:Ōtori-taisha]][[en:Ōtori taisha]][[fr:Ōtori-taisha]][[ja:大鳥大社]][[zh:大鳥大社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Ōtori-taisha]][[en:Ōtori taisha]][[fr:Ōtori-taisha]][[ja:大鳥大社]][[zh:大鳥大社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sakai, Osaka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōtori taisha
大鳥大社 | image = Ōtori-taisha, Worship Hall 001.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōtori Taisha {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|32|12.7|N|135|27|38.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji]] and {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} | founded_by = | established = c.{{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} | date_destroyed = | location = 1-2 Ōtorikita-machi 1-chō, Nishi-ku, Sakai-shi, Osaka-fu 593-8328, Japan | website = {{Official|1=https://www.ootoritaisha.jp}} | architecture_style = ''{{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori-zukuri|1=Ōtori-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441991}}'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōtori Shrine'''|大鳥大社|Ōtori Taisha}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nishi-ku, Sakai|en|Nishi-ku, Sakai|WD=Q1042499|lt=Nishi-ku}}, {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on August 13.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Shrine name and legend== The shrine has been called variously as {{nihongo|Ōtori-daimyōjin|大鳥大明神||}} or {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-Ōmiya|大鳥大神宮||}} in the past. Although {{nihongo| Ōtori Jinja|大鳥神社||}} is the correct name, “Ōtori Taisha” has gained widespread popular acceptance. According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} mythology. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', as the folk hero Yamato Takeru was returning home after his conquests in eastern Japan, he feel ill after blasphemy against the deity of {{ill|Mount Ibuki|en|Mount Ibuki|WD=Q2624360|lt=Mount Ibuki}} and died in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}. However, after he was buried in a [[kofun|Kofun]], a white heron emerged from the tomb and flew westward. It stopped at two places (and ''kofun'' were built at each location) before disappearing to the heavens. However, per the legend of this shrine, the white heron made one final stop at the {{nihongo|Chigusa-no-mori|千種森||}}, a forest in Izumi Province, where this shrine was later built.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ōtori Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}'''| 日本武尊|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} and folk hero * {{nihongo|'''[[Ame-no-Koyane|Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami]]'''|大鳥連祖神|}}, ancestor of the Fujiwara clan ==History== There is little documentary evidence and it is uncertain when this shrine was first built. Architecturally, the buildings are constructed in the {{nihongo|''Ōtori-zukuri''|大鳥造||}} which is believed to be a very ancient style of {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture}}, second only to the style of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]].http://i295.photobucket.com/albums/mm130/peacefulapricot/Otori%20Jinja%20and%20Sumiyoshi%20Taisha/P4200061.jpg {{Bare URL image|date=March 2022}} The first mention of the shrine in documentation comes from an entry in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}}'' dated 823 AD, in which the Imperial Court requested that the shrine pray for rain. The name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', either in connection with prayer srelating to weather, or promotions in the shrine's official status. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Izumi Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}} and as an indication of its importance, an imperial messenger was dispatched to attend its annual ''[[Niiname-no-Matsuri]]''. During this period, the shrine was under the control of {{nihongo|'''Shinpu-ji'''|神鳳寺||}}, a Buddhist temple said to have been founded by {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki}} in either 708 AD or 740 AD. The temple had close connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}} of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, branches of the shrine were built at many locations around the country, mostly in connection with ''[[shōen]]'' estates controlled by the Fujiwara, and around this time the shrine came to be called the ''ichinomiya'' of Izumi Province. During the premodern period, the main ''[[kami]]'' worshipped at the Ōtori-daimyōjin was [[Amaterasu]], although the shrine's connection with the Yamato Takeru myth was well-known, and Yamato Takeru was enshrined as a secondary ''kami''. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} confiscated the shrine's estates, valued at 1300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'', in 1575. The shrine was burned down, but was one of a series of shrines and temples rebuilt in 1602 by order of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|WD=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori}}. The shrine was destroyed again during the 1615 {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. In 1662, Shinpu-ji and the Ōtori-daimyōjin were reconstructed by the ''{{ill|Sakai bugyō|en|Sakai bugyō|WD=Q4274622|lt=Sakai bugyō}}'' per instructions by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and were repaired in 1701 by {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}. Under the patronage of Yanagisawa, the temple became the head temple of the "Shingon Risshū Southern School" and had 76 daughter temples, mainly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}. The shrine's fortunes waxed and waned together with the temple, although with the growth of ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' studies towards the [[:en:Bakumatsu period|Bakumatsu period]] there were increasing calls for the shrine's rehabilitation. This goal was realized with the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], when Shingu-ji was destroyed and Ōtori Jinja reemerged as a purely Shinto shrine. In 1871, the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|''Kanpei-taisha''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and in 1876, after reevaluation, the resident ''kami'' was changed from Amaterasu to {{nihongo| Ōtori-no-muraji-no-oyagami|大鳥連祖神||}}, who was identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the ancestor of the Fujiwara clan. This caused a backlash from the shrine; however, despite vehement ongoing protest, the ruling was upheld by the government in 1896. Ongoing efforts to make Yamato Takeru the resident ''kami'' were also ignored by the government, and it was not until 1961 that the shrine was able to install Yamato Takeru next to Ōtori-no-muraji. The main shrine building was destroyed by lightning on August 15, 1905, and the current ''[[Honden]]'' was rebuilt in December 1909. The shrine is five minutes on foot from [[:en:Otori Station|Otori Station]] on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hanwa Line|en|Hanwa Line|WD=Q1154800|lt=Hanwa Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Ōtori-taisha, A Torii Gate at the Entrance to a Shrine 001.jpg|A torii gate at the entrance of a shrine File:Ōtori-taisha, Principal Shrine 001.jpg|Honden File:Ōtori-taisha, Statue of Yamato Takeru 001.jpg|Statue of Yamato Takeru ==Festival== The shrine holds a ''{{ill|Danjiri Matsuri|en|Danjiri Matsuri|WD=Q5219928|lt=Danjiri Matsuri}}’' festival is held every year on the weekend of the first week of October. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine002.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine017.jpg|At grounds of Ōtori taisha. File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine016.jpg|A priest of Ōtori taisha prays for the safety of the festival. File:2019 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine001.jpg|Torii gate of the main entrance to Ōtori taisha File:2019 Danjiri festival at Otori Shrine003.jpg File:Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 2022 003.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 009.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 007.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 005.webm File:2022 Danjiri Festival at Otori Shrine in Sakai, Osaka 006.webm ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ōtori-taisha}} *[https://www.ootoritaisha.jp Official website] *[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/sakai/alacarte/jinja.html#outoritaisha Sakai City home page] *[https://osaka-info.jp/en/spot/otoritaisha/ Osaka Official Travel Guide] ‎ {{Ōtori Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Ōtori taisha]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-taisha}} [[fr:Ōtori-taisha]] [[ja:大鳥大社]] [[zh:大鳥大社]] {{wikidata link|Q705151}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Izumi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Sakai, Osaka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
